title: Why This Hostility Towards Sugarcane and Paddy Farmers?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The English translation and the Tamil reading copy below follow the printed source pages. Obvious OCR defects were corrected against those images, without altering the canonical source layer.
paragraphs: Ever since the AIADMK came to power in Tamil Nadu in May 2011, one problem after another has descended upon the State's farmers: failed crops, drought, inadequate credit, irregularities in crop insurance, discrimination in drought relief, and livestock stricken by foot-and-mouth disease. As the saying goes, the blows keep falling upon the already wounded. There seems to be no end to their suffering. Those in the ruling AIADMK, meanwhile, treat the farmers with indifference and turn a deaf ear to them. Is there even a Department of Agriculture? Does that department function under this government? In 2005-2006, during Jayalalithaa's rule, Tamil Nadu's foodgrain production was 6.146 million tonnes.

In 2009-2010, under the DMK government, foodgrain production stood at 9.56 million tonnes. It was the DMK government that waived approximately Rs. 7,000 crore in agricultural loans, thereby benefiting 2,240,739 farming families.

Paddy is cultivated over approximately 2.2 million hectares in Tamil Nadu. Among the Cauvery delta districts where paddy is grown, Tiruvarur, Nagapattinam and Thanjavur are entirely under such cultivation, while parts of Tiruchirappalli, Pudukkottai and Cuddalore are also paddy-growing areas. Many hundreds of thousands of agricultural labourers who once worked in these delta districts have now begun taking up other occupations for their livelihood, in brick kilns, Tiruppur export firms and building construction. Consequently, labourers are no longer available for agricultural work in the delta districts. In 1981, a quintal of paddy in these districts fetched Rs. 120. At the time, if a husband and wife came together for farm work, their combined wage was Rs. 12. The proceeds from selling one quintal of paddy could therefore pay ten pairs of workers. But what is the position today? A quintal of paddy now fetches Rs. 1,360. Farmers say that the proceeds from one quintal are not enough to pay even three pairs of workers.

Higher rents are demanded for tractors used to plough the fields and for machinery used in planting and harvesting. When asked why, they cite the rise in diesel prices. The prices of inputs such as seed paddy, fertiliser and pesticides have also risen to unprecedented levels. At a time when cultivation costs and agricultural input prices have increased so greatly, farmers are filled with expectation and anxiety over whether paddy will receive a remunerative price.

When the DMK came to power for the fifth time in 2006-2007, the price per quintal was fixed at Rs. 650 for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 620 for ordinary-grade paddy. These prices were raised step by step, and by 2009-2010 the price had been increased to Rs. 1,100 for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 1,050 for ordinary-grade paddy.

For this year, the Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation has announced a procurement price of Rs. 1,415 per quintal for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 1,360 for common-grade paddy, after adding State subsidies of Rs. 70 and Rs. 50 respectively to the Central government's minimum support price. Speaking to reporters about this situation, Arupathi Kalyanam, general secretary of the Federation of Farmers' Associations of Thanjavur, Tiruvarur and Nagapattinam districts, said: “Last year, severe drought caused all the cultivated crops to wither and burn, inflicting losses upon farmers. This year, because the Mettur dam did not have sufficient water, water was not released at the proper time for kuruvai cultivation. Farmers have therefore undertaken samba cultivation with all their hopes placed upon it. But they are worried that the price offered for paddy is inadequate. The Tamil Nadu government should provide a procurement price of at least Rs. 2,000 per quintal.”

Sugarcane farmers, too, have continually raised their voices because they are not receiving an adequate procurement price. In 2005-2006, under the AIADMK government, the price had been fixed at Rs. 1,014 per tonne. In 2010-2011, under the DMK government, it was raised to Rs. 2,000 per tonne. But what is the situation now? Sugarcane is cultivated annually over 865,000 acres in Tamil Nadu. The government has set a target of raising this year's sugarcane production to 50 million tonnes. Tamil Nadu has 46 sugar mills in all: 16 in the cooperative sector, three in the public sector and 27 in the private sector. Except for three mills, all the others are operating.

During the DMK government's tenure, a tripartite meeting involving farmers, Agriculture Department officials and sugar mill managements was held every year in Tamil Nadu. The sugarcane procurement price was fixed according to the decision reached at that meeting. Under the present government, however, there is no room for democratic consultation, and so the tripartite meeting itself is not held. The procurement price for sugarcane is fixed unilaterally, purely on the authority of the government.

Last year, Tamil Nadu fixed the procurement price for one tonne of sugarcane at Rs. 2,350. Of this, Rs. 150 is deducted as transport charges. Sugar mill managements deposit only the remaining Rs. 2,200 into farmers' bank accounts. Since last year, sugarcane farmers have lamented that, amid rising input costs, a shortage of labour and scarcity of water, the procurement price offered for sugarcane is not remunerative. Paddy farmers similarly shed tears because the price offered for their crop is inadequate.

The sugarcane season begins in August each year. It is customary for sugarcane farmers to demand a higher procurement price at that time. Until last season, farmers had demanded a field price of Rs. 3,500 per tonne of sugarcane. But the government took no notice of that demand. Last year, they demanded at least Rs. 2,500 per tonne, in keeping with what the AIADMK had stated in its election manifesto two years earlier. Yet the Tamil Nadu government did not come forward to implement even this.

Now that the sugarcane season has begun, farmers are asking for Rs. 4,000 per tonne. But with the parliamentary election fast approaching, the Tamil Nadu government hastily announced yesterday that the price of sugarcane would be fixed at Rs. 2,650 per tonne.

Furthermore, although the Central government has adequate sugar stocks, it has not banned sugar imports. Nor has it come forward to raise the import duty on sugar. In Uttar Pradesh, the sugarcane price is Rs. 2,800 per tonne, and farmers there are agitating for even that price to be raised. In Tamil Nadu, however, an even lower price is being offered. Four months have passed since the sugarcane season began. Only now have they hurriedly announced the procurement price here, with the approaching parliamentary election in mind.

All the government-supporting newspapers have carried the news. In particular, the daily *Dina Thanthi* published the very headline: “In the interests of farmers, recommended sugarcane price raised by Rs. 550 per tonne, announces Jayalalithaa.” Until now, the price had been fixed at Rs. 2,350 per tonne of sugarcane. If, as the government's news says, the increase is Rs. 550 per tonne, what should the newly fixed price be? Anyone would naturally add Rs. 550 to Rs. 2,350 and conclude that it should be Rs. 2,900. But the Tamil Nadu government has now fixed it at only Rs. 2,650, inclusive of the Central government's price. In other words, it has raised last year's price by a mere Rs. 300, while trying to deceive sugarcane farmers by claiming an increase of Rs. 550.

To explain further, when the Central government fixed the price at Rs. 1,450 per tonne for the 2011-2012 sugarcane season, the Tamil Nadu government added Rs. 650 and fixed the price at Rs. 2,100 per tonne. Likewise, when the Central government fixed the 2012-2013 sugarcane price at Rs. 1,700 per tonne, the Tamil Nadu government again added Rs. 650 and fixed it at Rs. 2,350.

For the past two years after the AIADMK came to power, it had added Rs. 650 per tonne. This year, as though to crush sugarcane farmers still further, it has not increased the price even by that Rs. 650; it has reduced the addition by Rs. 100 and announced only Rs. 550. Even this figure of Rs. 550 merely represents the amount above the price fixed by the Central government. The truth is that the State government has raised its own price from last year's Rs. 2,350 by only Rs. 300. Concealing this, they seek to deceive sugarcane farmers by publishing reports as though the price had been increased by Rs. 550 this year. Even *Theekkathir*, the newspaper of the Communist Party of India (Marxist), which supports the AIADMK, reported this truth today, 23 December 2013, under the headline: “Sugarcane price cut by Rs. 100, farmers protest.” P. Shanmugam, general secretary of the Tamil Nadu Farmers' Association, has called for strong protest movements against the Tamil Nadu government's announcement on sugarcane pricing, which deceives farmers. Likewise, N. Palanisamy, State president of the Tamil Nadu Sugarcane Farmers' Association, and its general secretary T. Ravindran have issued a statement condemning the price now fixed by the Tamil Nadu government as unremunerative.

Speaking to reporters about this increase, Rajkumar, working president of the South Indian Sugarcane Farmers' Association, said: “The sugarcane procurement price announced by the Tamil Nadu government has shocked us. Over these three years, farmers in Tamil Nadu have been severely affected by rising prices and power shortages. Ignoring all this, the Tamil Nadu government has announced a sugarcane price that strikes at the stomachs of the poor. The government must therefore reconsider the price and raise it to Rs. 3,500 per tonne.” Viruthagiri, general secretary of the Consortium of Indian Farmers, further expressed his anguish: “The government has not raised the sugarcane procurement price to the expected level. Bowing to pressure from sugar mill managements, it has deceived the farmers.”

It was with an understanding of the hardships faced by paddy and sugarcane farmers that, at the DMK General Council meeting held recently, we adopted among our resolutions the following: “This government must come forward to fix and provide a remunerative procurement price of Rs. 2,000 per quintal for paddy farmers, above the cost of production; and this General Council strongly urges the government to provide a sugarcane procurement price of at least Rs. 3,500 per tonne.”

But what does it matter who says it? What does it matter who asks? In that spirit, they have ignored the words of farmers' association leaders, ignored what the opposition parties have said, failed to convene the tripartite meeting, and unilaterally fixed and announced the price. I believe that in the approaching parliamentary election, the voters of Tamil Nadu, including paddy and sugarcane farmers, will teach the ruling party an appropriate lesson for behaving in this arbitrary manner.
Affectionately,
M.K.
24 December 2013
